issues.description: FR-2 says 'reads it via env!("BUILD_SHA") at compile time; falls back to "unknown" if the env var wasn't set'. This is contradictory: env!() is a compile-time macro that causes a compilation FAILURE if the variable is missing — it cannot fall back. The correct Rust idiom is option_env!("BUILD_SHA").unwrap_or("unknown"). An implementer following the spec literally would either use env!() and break dev builds, or silently deviate from the spec.
issues.suggestion: Replace 'reads it via env!("BUILD_SHA")' with 'reads it via option_env!("BUILD_SHA").unwrap_or("unknown")' to match the stated fallback behavior.

issues.description: The Overview section references env!("VERGEN_GIT_SHA_SHORT") and a 'vergen-like crate', but FR-2 switches to a plain build.rs with BUILD_SHA. Two different env var names and two different mechanisms are described. An implementer must guess which is normative.
issues.suggestion: Remove the vergen reference from the Overview or explicitly state 'the Overview mentions vergen as prior art; the normative approach is the plain build.rs in FR-2 using BUILD_SHA'.

issues.description: NFR-2 says 'Update the existing health_returns_ok_json_when_store_healthy test' but the actual test name in the codebase is test_health_returns_json_ok. A second test test_health_returns_degraded_on_db_failure also constructs the health response and would need updating for build_info. The spec doesn't mention the degraded-path test.
issues.suggestion: Reference both existing tests by their actual names, or say 'update all existing health tests to assert build_info presence'.

issues.description: FR-1 says '7-character short SHA' and AC says '7-char string'. git rev-parse --short HEAD defaults to 7 but can produce more characters in repos with hash prefix collisions. This is unlikely to matter but the spec is slightly imprecise.
issues.suggestion: Say 'short SHA (typically 7 characters)' or pass --short=7 explicitly in the build.rs command to guarantee exactly 7.
